cases.input: chat icon
cases.note: Decorative icon with alt text. ACC-05 says decorative images should have empty alt. Tests whether 'chat icon' is descriptive enough or just restating the visual.
cases.human_notes: This appears to be a decorative chat icon that should have empty alt text per ACC-05. 'Chat icon' just restates the visual without adding meaningful information for screen reader users who can't see the icon anyway. Confidence is low because this is a novel content type combination and the functional vs decorative distinction requires more context.

cases.input: Shopify icon
cases.note: Brand logo alt text. 'Shopify icon' describes what it looks like, not what it does. Better alt: 'Shopify integration'. Should fail if empty
cases.human_notes: Alt text describes the visual appearance ('icon') rather than the functional purpose. For a clickable brand logo that likely navigates to Shopify integration, this provides no meaningful context to screen reader users about what the link does.

cases.input: capterra badge
cases.note: Review badge alt text. 'capterra badge' doesn't convey the information the badge communicates (e.g., a rating). Missing the point of alt text.
cases.human_notes: Generic 'capterra badge' doesn't convey what achievement or rating the badge represents, which is the functional information users need. Should specify the award type or rating value the badge communicates.

cases.input: G2 Logo Red RGB 1
cases.note: Alt text that looks like a filename, not a description. Classic ACC-05 failure — someone copied the asset name instead of writing real alt text.
cases.human_notes: This is clearly a filename used as alt text rather than a description of what users need to know. However, this is the first ACC-05 case in the dataset, so confidence is low despite the obvious violation.

cases.input: Mailchimp marketing automation flow showing integration between Canva, Shopify, and Wix platforms for abandoned cart recovery email campaign
cases.note: Good descriptive alt text for a complex image. Should pass — describes the content and context.
cases.human_notes: Describes the marketing automation flow diagram with specific platforms and campaign purpose. This is a novel combination (ACC-05 + alt_text) with no precedent in the dataset, making confidence assessment difficult.

cases.input: Mobile phone screen showing a text message sent via Mailchimp's omnichannel marketing tools
cases.note: Descriptive alt text for a product screenshot. Tests whether the checker recognizes good alt text.
cases.human_notes: Describes the visual content and provides product context without being overly verbose. This is a novel combination (ACC-05 + alt_text) with no precedent in the dataset, so confidence is low despite the clear pass verdict.

cases.input: Data-driven marketing without the complexity
cases.note: H1 hero heading. Tests plain language in a marketing context — 'data-driven' is borderline jargon.
cases.human_notes: The term 'data-driven' is widely understood in the target audience of marketing professionals and business owners. It's descriptive rather than gratuitously technical, and the phrase effectively contrasts complexity with simplicity to communicate the value proposition.

cases.input: Businesses like yours are growing faster—and stressing less
cases.note: Second-person address in a heading ('yours'). Also uses an em dash. Tests VT-02 in heading context.
cases.human_notes: Uses conversational second-person ('yours') and focuses on relatable business outcomes rather than Mailchimp features. The em dash creates natural rhythm. Appropriate tone for marketing headlines targeting small business owners.

cases.input: Marketing that delivers results
cases.note: Generic marketing heading. Tests whether the checker flags vague claims as a CLR-01 issue or lets marketing copy through.
cases.human_notes: Generic marketing claim without specificity - 'delivers results' could mean anything from increased sales to better engagement rates. The audience (potential customers evaluating marketing tools) needs concrete information about what kind of results to expect, not vague promises.

cases.input: 97% more clicks
cases.note: Numeral usage in a heading. Clean pass for GRM-05.
cases.human_notes: Uses numerals correctly for a statistic (97%), follows sentence case, and clearly communicates a quantifiable benefit. Standard application of GRM-05 for numerical data in headings.

cases.input: See up to 30x ROI
cases.note: Hedging in a heading ('up to'). Tests VT-04 in marketing context — is 'up to' necessary qualification or hedge?
cases.human_notes: 'Up to' hedges the claim without providing meaningful qualification. The phrase promises maximum 30x ROI but gives no indication of typical results or conditions required. This is hedging language that obscures rather than clarifies the value proposition.

cases.input: Get started easily with a personalized product tour
cases.note: Verb-first heading with clear action. Tests ACT-01 on a longer heading.
cases.human_notes: Starts with a clear verb ('Get started') and tells the user what they'll accomplish. The heading is action-oriented and specific about the benefit (easy start with personalized tour).

cases.input: Try our Standard plan for free!
cases.note: Exclamation mark in a heading. GRM-03 says avoid exclamation marks. Tests on marketing copy where they're common.
cases.human_notes: The exclamation mark violates GRM-03's guidance to avoid them. While marketing copy often uses exclamation marks for enthusiasm, the standard applies to all content types including headings. This is a novel combination with no precedent, making confidence low.

cases.input: Get 15% off our Standard plan
cases.note: Verb-first heading. Clean pass for ACT-01.
cases.human_notes: Starts with a verb ('Get'), clearly states the action and benefit. This is a promotional heading that effectively communicates what the user will receive.

cases.input: Save 15% on the ultimate expert-assisted platform for growth
cases.note: Marketing superlatives ('ultimate'). Tests CLR-01 plain language on marketing copy.
cases.human_notes: 'Ultimate expert-assisted platform' uses vague marketing language that doesn't help users understand what they're actually buying. The superlative 'ultimate' and buzzword 'platform for growth' obscure rather than clarify the product offering.

cases.input: Millions of users trust us with their email marketing. You can too.
cases.note: Two-sentence heading. Tests whether the checker applies sentence length rules to headings.
cases.human_notes: Two sentences totaling 14 words, well under CLR-03's 20-word guidance for headings. The content is clear and scannable. This is a novel combination (CLR-03 + heading) with no precedent, so confidence is low despite the straightforward application.

cases.input: Plus support for 300+ Integrations
cases.note: Title case on 'Integrations' in a heading. Tests CON-02.
cases.human_notes: Uses title case on 'Integrations' when sentence case would be 'Plus support for 300+ integrations.' This is not a proper noun requiring capitalization, just a common noun describing software connections.

cases.input: No matter what your expertise level, Mailchimp is an affordable way to fuel small business growth.
cases.note: Plain language test. 'expertise level' is borderline jargon. Marketing body copy.
cases.human_notes: Marketing copy uses accessible language throughout. 'expertise level' is common parlance, not jargon — users readily understand it as 'how much you know.' The sentence structure is simple and the benefit is clear.

cases.input: Grow your list, learn about your audience, and send personalized, high-impact campaigns.
cases.note: Oxford comma in a 3-item list. Clean pass for GRM-01.
cases.human_notes: Uses Oxford comma correctly in a three-item list with parallel verb phrases. Clear example of proper GRM-01 compliance.

cases.input: Dedicated onboarding support and migration tools make the switch to Mailchimp seamless.
cases.note: Tech jargon ('onboarding', 'migration tools') in consumer-facing copy. Tests CLR-01.
cases.human_notes: Onboarding and migration are important parts of an email marketing tool. It's wise to bring them to people's attention and these word choices explain the product and the value props in a concise way. This is good content design.

cases.input: Join Mailchimp with a free 14-day trial or save 15% off on 10,000+ contacts.
cases.note: Numerals in marketing copy (14, 15%). Tests GRM-05.
cases.human_notes: Marketing copy correctly uses numerals for quantities (14-day, 15%, 10,000+) rather than spelling them out. This follows standard marketing conventions and improves scannability for promotional content.

cases.input: Join Mailchimp with a risk-free 14-day trial—no credit card required.
cases.note: Contains 'risk-free' — not an idiom but a compound modifier. Tests TRN-04 boundary.
cases.human_notes: 'Risk-free' is a standard compound modifier in marketing copy, not an idiom that would confuse translators. The hyphen is grammatically correct and the term translates clearly across languages as 'without risk' or equivalent constructions.

cases.input: Use email & SMS to maximize ROI with intuitive marketing analytics, automations, and AI tools.
cases.note: Ampersand in body copy. GRM-04 should flag this — it's not a heading or nav element.
cases.human_notes: The ampersand appears in marketing copy where 'and' would be more appropriate for readability. GRM-04 applies to maintain professional tone in longer-form content.

cases.input: Customers saw up to 97% higher click rates when they used both email & SMS.*
cases.note: Hedging with 'up to' in a stat claim. Tests VT-04 — is this necessary qualification for a stat?
cases.human_notes: The 'up to' qualifier is necessary for a statistic that represents the highest performance observed across varied customer scenarios. Without it, '97% higher click rates' would imply all customers achieved exactly that result, which would be misleading. The hedging language makes the claim accurate rather than deceptive.

cases.input: E-commerce businesses see up to 30x ROI* using Mailchimp’s Standard Plan.
cases.note: Another 'up to' hedge with an asterisk. Tests VT-04 and whether asterisks affect the assessment.
cases.human_notes: The asterisk references additional details about the claim, which is a standard practice for marketing statistics. The core message 'E-commerce businesses see up to 30x ROI using Mailchimp's Standard Plan' remains clear and understandable.

cases.input: Our customers have sent over 9.8 billion emails with AI-generated content.*
cases.note: Large numeral (9.8 billion). Tests GRM-05 on big numbers.
cases.human_notes: Uses '9.8 billion' instead of '9,800,000,000' which is exactly what GRM-05 requires for readability. The asterisk indicates sourcing/methodology context, which is appropriate for statistical claims.

cases.input: An onboarding specialist is here to help you get started with confidence—it’s included with Standard and Premium plans.*
cases.note: Passive-ish construction. Tests VT-01 on marketing copy that's warm but slightly indirect.
cases.human_notes: While the construction is passive ('specialist is here' rather than 'specialist will help you'), it's intentionally conversational and reassuring. VT-01 targets truly weak passive voice that obscures agency, not stylistic choices that prioritize warmth over directness in marketing contexts.

cases.input: For 24 years, Mailchimp has helped millions of businesses get more opens, clicks, and sales.
cases.note: Numeral (24) in body copy. Clean pass for GRM-05.
cases.human_notes: Uses '24 years' rather than spelling out 'twenty-four years' which is correct for marketing copy where the number emphasizes scale and credibility. GRM-05 allows numerals for quantities that would be cumbersome to spell out.

cases.input: Films, podcasts, and original series that celebrate the entrepreneurial spirit. Check it out
cases.note: 'Entrepreneurial spirit' is borderline idiom. Tests TRN-04.
cases.human_notes: 'Entrepreneurial spirit' is an idiom that may not translate clearly across cultures. If the content is localized to western markets, this is an easy pass. If the content is NOT localized to western markets, this is an easy fail. It's a tough one.

cases.input: Need to manage more contacts? Get in touch to learn about custom plans.
cases.note: Question + action. Tests ACT-04 for clear next steps.
cases.human_notes: The copy provides a clear next step ('Get in touch to learn about custom plans') with specific context about what the user will learn. While 'get in touch' could be more specific, it's paired with the specific outcome 'to learn about custom plans' which satisfies ACT-04's requirement for clear next steps.

cases.input: Engage your audience and increase sales with 15% off your first 12 months†. Plus, get expert support to help migrate your data and provide strategic guidance—exclusive to the Premium plan.
cases.note: Verb-first marketing copy with action words.
cases.human_notes: Contains action verbs ('Engage,' 'increase,' 'get,' 'help,' 'migrate,' 'provide') and clearly communicates what the user will achieve. The compound structure doesn't undermine the action-oriented messaging that ACT-01 requires.

cases.input: Find out why customers see up to 24x ROI* using the Standard plan with a risk-free 14-day trial†. Cancel or downgrade to our Essentials or basic Free plans at any time.
cases.note: Buries the lede — starts with 'find out why' instead of leading with the value. Tests CLR-02.
cases.human_notes: This is marketing copy doing what marketing copy does. They are intentionally burying the lead and going for a big hook.

cases.input: Start Today.
cases.note: Period at end of a CTA button. Unusual. Tests whether the checker flags punctuation on buttons.
cases.human_notes: Button CTAs should not end with punctuations. This fails on multiple levels, title case, punctuation, and should not have passed in the preprocessing phase.

cases.input: Contact sales
cases.note: Verb-first CTA. Clean pass.
cases.human_notes: Starts with a verb ('Contact'), clearly communicates the action. Standard verb-first CTA pattern with no ambiguity about what happens when clicked.

cases.input: Buy Now Buy Now
cases.note: Duplicated button text — extraction artifact or actual bug. Tests content structure.
cases.human_notes: The duplicated text creates redundancy that serves no structural purpose and would confuse users. While STR-01 focuses on parallel structure in lists, the underlying principle of clear, non-redundant structure applies here.

cases.input: Hi, %s
cases.note: Unresolved template variable in a button. Edge case — broken content, not a standards issue.
cases.human_notes: This is broken templating code, not content that violates CLR-01. The %s placeholder indicates a technical implementation issue where the user's name failed to render. CLR-01 evaluates plain language clarity, but this isn't functional content —. This needs engineering attention, not editorial revision.

cases.input: Skip to main content
cases.note: Skip navigation link. ACC-02 is about writing for screen readers. This is a standard a11y pattern.
cases.human_notes: Standard accessibility skip link that clearly tells screen readers what action will happen and where they'll land. Uses proper sentence case and action verb structure that ACC-02 requires for assistive technology.

cases.input: Case Studies
cases.note: Title case in nav. Tests CON-02.
cases.human_notes: This is title case, not sentence case. Should be 'Case studies' per CON-02. The machine incorrectly identified this as sentence case when both words are capitalized.

cases.input: Free Marketing Tools
cases.note: Title case in footer nav. Tests CON-02.
cases.human_notes: Navigation labels should use sentence case. This capitalizes all major words when only 'Free' should be capitalized, creating inconsistency with standard UI conventions.

cases.input: Give Where You Live
cases.note: Title case in footer. Also borderline idiom (TRN-04).
cases.human_notes: Title case is incorrect for a UI label. Even though this appears in the footer, it's still a navigational element that should follow sentence case conventions, not marketing headline rules.

cases.input: Learn about omnichannel marketing
cases.note: Descriptive link text. Should pass ACC-01 — describes the destination.
cases.human_notes: Clearly indicates what the user will learn about when they click. 'Omnichannel marketing' provides sufficient context for screen readers and users alike.

cases.input: See what Mailchimp can do for e-commerce
cases.note: Descriptive link text. Should pass ACC-01.
cases.human_notes: The link text clearly describes what the user will find on the destination page. A screen reader would announce 'See what Mailchimp can do for e-commerce' which provides clear context about the content behind the link.

cases.input: Find out how easy it is to switch
cases.note: Descriptive but vague link text. Borderline ACC-01 — 'switch' to what?
cases.human_notes: The link text 'Find out how easy it is to switch' is acceptable in the context of an email marketiing platform

cases.input: Talk to Sales at +1 (800) 330-4838
cases.note: Descriptive link with phone number. Passes ACC-01.
cases.human_notes: The link text clearly describes both the action ('Talk to Sales') and provides the specific phone number.

cases.input: Select the Standard plan
cases.note: Verb-first CTA label. Clean pass.
cases.human_notes: Starts with action verb 'Select' and clearly indicates what will happen. Standard plan appears to be a product tier name requiring proper capitalization.

cases.input: X (formerly Twitter)
cases.note: Brand name transition pattern. Tests whether the checker handles parenthetical clarifications.
cases.human_notes: The capitalization follows standard brand naming conventions for both the current brand (X) and former brand (Twitter).

cases.input: Try for free Try risk-free
cases.note: Duplicated label text with slight variation. Extraction artifact exposing inconsistent microcopy.
cases.human_notes: Two nearly identical CTAs concatenated creates redundancy that weakens the message structure. This is an extraction artifact, but the underlying issue is inconsistent microcopy that would confuse users about the specific action they're taking.

cases.input: This page is now available in other languages.
cases.note: Translation-aware UI copy. TRN-02 is about writing for translation. Meta case — the UI itself addresses localization.
cases.human_notes: The copy is clear, uses function words appropriately, and avoids cultural assumptions. While it's meta (UI about localization), it still needs to follow translation-ready principles, which it does. No precedent exists for TRN-02 + ui_label combinations.

cases.input: Save 15% on 10,000+ contacts
cases.note: Numerals in a label (15%, 10,000+). Tests GRM-05.
cases.human_notes: Uses numerals appropriately for percentages and quantities, follows sentence case, and clearly communicates the promotional offer. No GRM-05 violations.

cases.input: Contacts Contacts
cases.note: Duplicated label. Extraction artifact or real duplication in the UI.
cases.human_notes: The duplication creates a confusing label that doesn't serve users. While the extraction note suggests this might be an artifact, STR-01 evaluates content as presented, and 'Contacts Contacts' fails to clearly identify the UI element's purpose.

cases.input: Personalized onboarding: Onboarding services differ per plan and are available for new or upgraded users with a Standard or Premium plan for the first 90 days after account creation or upgrade. Onboarding services are currently offered in English, Spanish and Portuguese for Premium plans, and in English for Standard plans.
cases.note: Long disclaimers. Tests CLR-01 on fine print. Also tests whether the checker flags 'personalized onboarding' as jargon.
cases.human_notes: Legal disclaimer text explaining service limitations and availability. While dense, it clearly states who gets what services, when, and in which languages. CLR-01's exception for audiences that require technical detail applies to legal disclaimers where precision matters more than simplicity.

cases.input: ©2001-2025 All Rights Reserved. Mailchimp® is a registered trademark of The Rocket Science Group. Apple and the Apple logo are trademarks of Apple Inc. Mac App Store is a service mark of Apple Inc. Google Play and the Google Play logo are trademarks of Google Inc. | | |
cases.note: Legal/copyright boilerplate. Same pattern as Kaiser's legal footer — should be routed as legal content.
cases.human_notes: Legal boilerplate required for trademark and copyright protection. Like the Kaiser example, this serves a compliance function for lawyers and trademark offices, not user communication. CLR-01's exception for audiences that require specific language applies to mandatory legal notices.
